producedFindings.title: slack service imported but never used after removal of slack.listen()
producedFindings.description: The slack service is still imported at the top of initServices(), but slack.listen() was removed from the initialization flow. This means the slack service is loaded but never initialized, which could be an oversight or leave the service in a broken state.
producedFindings.recommendation: Either remove the slack service import if it's no longer needed, or ensure slack is properly initialized with the appropriate method call.

producedFindings.title: scheduling.init() called without required apiUrl parameter
producedFindings.description: The scheduling.init() call was changed from scheduling.init({ apiUrl: urlUtils.urlFor('api', {type: 'admin'}, true) }) to scheduling.init() with no arguments. If the scheduling service requires the apiUrl parameter to function correctly, this will cause runtime errors or misbehavior.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify whether scheduling.init() can function without the apiUrl parameter. If the parameter is still required, restore it. If the scheduling service API has changed to not require it, ensure the service implementation has been updated accordingly.

producedFindings.title: emailAddressService.init() moved into Promise.all without dependency verification
producedFindings.description: emailAddressService.init() was previously awaited separately before the Promise.all block, with a comment noting that 'newsletter service and email service depend on email address service'. Moving it into Promise.all means it now runs in parallel with other services that may depend on it, potentially causing race conditions.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that no services in the Promise.all block depend on emailAddressService being initialized. If dependencies exist, keep emailAddressService.init() as a separate await before Promise.all.

groundTruth.category: Package Manager Must Be Yarn v1
groundTruth.title: NPM referenced instead of Yarn
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all package management commands to use yarn, but the code documentation references npm for dependency installation

groundTruth.title: Slack notifications not functioning - event listeners never registered
groundTruth.description: The slack.listen() call was removed from the service initialization in Promise.all, but the slack service module is still imported. This means the Slack service event listeners for 'post.published' and 'slack.test' events are never registered during boot. As a result, posts published on the site will not trigger Slack webhook notifications even when Slack integration is configured correctly. The slack.listen() method registers critical event handlers that connect the Ghost event system to the Slack notification service, and without this call, the integration is completely non-functional.

groundTruth.title: Post scheduling will fail on boot - missing required apiUrl parameter
groundTruth.description: The scheduling.init() call is missing the required apiUrl parameter that was previously passed via urlUtils.urlFor('api', {type: 'admin'}, true). The PostScheduler constructor explicitly checks for this parameter and throws an IncorrectUsageError if it's not provided. This will cause Ghost to crash during the boot sequence when attempting to initialize the scheduling service. The apiUrl is critical for the scheduling service as it's used to generate signed admin tokens and callback URLs for scheduled post publishing. Without this parameter, the scheduled publishing feature cannot function.

groundTruth.title: Race condition in email service initialization - dependency order violated
groundTruth.description: The emailAddressService.init() was moved from sequential await execution into the parallel Promise.all block alongside emailService.init(). The comment explicitly states 'newsletter service and email service depend on email address service', indicating a required initialization order. The EmailServiceWrapper's init() method imports and uses emailAddressService, expecting it to be fully initialized. By running these initializations in parallel, emailService may attempt to access emailAddressService before it has completed initialization, leading to undefined behavior, null reference errors, or incorrect email configuration. This race condition can cause intermittent failures that are difficult to reproduce and debug.

producedFindings.title: slack.listen() call removed without explanation
producedFindings.description: The slack.listen() call was removed from the service initialization, but there's no indication that the slack service itself was removed or that this removal was intentional. This could break Slack integration functionality.
producedFindings.recommendation: If slack service removal is intentional, also remove the slack service import. If not, restore the slack.listen() call.

producedFindings.title: scheduling.init() no longer receives required apiUrl parameter
producedFindings.description: The scheduling.init() call previously received an apiUrl parameter constructed via urlUtils.urlFor('api', {type: 'admin'}, true). Removing this parameter may break the scheduling service if it requires the API URL for its initialization.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that scheduling.init() no longer requires the apiUrl parameter, or restore the parameter if it's still needed.

producedFindings.title: emailAddressService.init() moved into Promise.all without dependency verification
producedFindings.description: The comment states 'newsletter service and email service depend on email address service', and previously emailAddressService.init() was awaited separately before Promise.all. Now it's included in Promise.all, which means emailService.init() could start before emailAddressService.init() completes, potentially causing race conditions.
producedFindings.recommendation: Keep emailAddressService.init() as a separate await before Promise.all to ensure it completes before emailService.init() starts, or verify that the dependency has been refactored.

producedFindings.title: Potential null pointer access on openForm
producedFindings.description: The optional chaining operator was removed from `openForm.in_reply_to_snippet`, changing from `!!openForm?.in_reply_to_snippet` to `!!openForm.in_reply_to_snippet`. If `openForm` can be null or undefined, this will throw a runtime error.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that `openForm` is guaranteed to be non-null when this code path is executed (when `isOpen` is true). If there's any possibility of `openForm` being null, restore the optional chaining operator.

groundTruth.category: Tailwind CSS Classes Must Follow Standard Ordering
groundTruth.title: Tailwind classes in incorrect order
groundTruth.description: Tailwind utility classes must follow standard ordering: layout, positioning, sizing, spacing (margin before padding), typography, visual effects. The classes have padding (pr-2) before margin (mt-[-16px]), which violates this convention.

groundTruth.title: Missing optional chaining causes runtime error when openForm is undefined
groundTruth.description: The code accesses `openForm.in_reply_to_snippet` without optional chaining, but openForm can be undefined when rendering certain forms (like the main comment form). The original code correctly used `openForm?.in_reply_to_snippet` with optional chaining. This will cause a "Cannot read property 'in_reply_to_snippet' of undefined" runtime error when the FormWrapper is rendered without an openForm prop, crashing the component.

groundTruth.title: Editor editability incorrectly depends on member expertise instead of name
groundTruth.description: The Form component determines whether the editor should be editable by checking `member?.expertise` instead of `memberName` (which is derived from `member?.name`). According to the comment and the original logic, the editor should be disabled only when the member doesn't have a name or when submitting. Expertise is optional metadata, but name is required for posting. This bug prevents users without expertise from typing in the comment editor, even though they have a valid name and should be allowed to comment.

groundTruth.title: Reply threading broken for nested comments due to incorrect in_reply_to_id
groundTruth.description: The submit function uses `parent.id` as `in_reply_to_id` instead of `openForm.in_reply_to_id`. For nested replies (replying to a reply), these values differ: `openForm.in_reply_to_id` points to the specific comment being replied to, while `parent.id` points to the top-level parent comment. Using `parent.id` breaks the reply chain, causing nested replies to be incorrectly threaded to the wrong comment. This destroys the conversation hierarchy and makes it impossible to track which comment a user is actually replying to in multi-level discussions.

producedFindings.title: Filter change may include today's emails in warmup calculation
producedFindings.description: The filter changed from `<` to `<=` when querying for emails before today. With `<=`, emails created at exactly midnight (00:00:00) of the current day would be included, which could cause the warmup limit calculation to include emails from today rather than only previous days. The original logic explicitly excluded today's emails to calculate the warmup limit based on historical data only.
producedFindings.recommendation: Revert to using `<` instead of `<=` to ensure today's emails are excluded from the warmup calculation, or add a comment explaining why including the current day's start is intentional.

producedFindings.title: Threshold comparison logic may skip correct scaling tier
producedFindings.description: The threshold comparison changed from `<=` to `<`. Combined with the sorted thresholds (1000, 5000, 100000, 400000), this creates a gap: when lastCount equals exactly 1000, 5000, or 100000, none of the threshold conditions match (since 1000 is not < 1000), and the code falls through to the fallback. For example, lastCount=100000 would skip the 1.75× scaling and use the fallback 1.2× instead.
producedFindings.recommendation: Change the comparison back to `<=` to ensure boundary values are handled correctly, or adjust the threshold limits to account for the exclusive comparison.

producedFindings.title: Test helper uses incorrect method name
producedFindings.description: The test setup uses `findAll` in createModelClass, but the actual service calls `findPage`. This mismatch could cause tests to not properly mock the service's behavior, though it may work if createModelClass handles both methods.
producedFindings.recommendation: Update the test setup to use `findPage` consistently with the service implementation: `findPage: {data: []}` instead of `findAll: []`.

producedFindings.title: Integration test has incorrect expected value comment
producedFindings.description: The comment states 250 × 1.25 = 313, but mathematically 250 × 1.25 = 312.5, which rounds up to 313 with Math.ceil. The comment is slightly misleading as it doesn't show the rounding step.
producedFindings.recommendation: Update the comment to show the calculation more accurately: `// Day 3: Math.ceil(250 × 1.25) = 313`

groundTruth.category: Code Must Use Strict Equality Operators
groundTruth.title: Non-strict inequality operator used
groundTruth.description: The rule requires using strict equality operators (=== or !==) for all comparisons, but the code uses != instead of !== to check if count is null

groundTruth.title: Date filter includes today's emails instead of excluding them
groundTruth.description: The filter in #getHighestCount() uses `created_at:<=` which includes today's date, but the docstring explicitly states it should exclude today. This causes the method to potentially return today's partial email count instead of the previous day's complete count. When emails are sent early in the day, this will result in a lower base count for warmup limit calculations, causing the domain warming progression to restart or regress unexpectedly. This breaks the intended day-over-day scaling behavior where each day's limit should be based on the previous day's complete sending volume.

groundTruth.title: High-volume threshold boundary applies wrong scaling factor at 400k
groundTruth.description: The condition uses `>=` instead of `>` when checking if lastCount qualifies for high-volume capping at exactly 400,000 emails. According to the documented scaling table, 400k should use the 2× multiplier from the thresholds array, but with this bug it immediately applies the high-volume cap of 1.2× or +75k absolute increase. For a site hitting exactly 400k messages, this means getting a limit of 480k (1.2×) or 475k (+75k) instead of the expected 800k (2×). This creates a significant bottleneck at a critical growth milestone and breaks the documented warmup progression.

groundTruth.title: Threshold boundary values skip their designated scaling tier
groundTruth.description: The loop uses `<` instead of `<=` when comparing lastCount to threshold limits. This causes exact boundary values (1000, 5000, 100000, 400000) to incorrectly skip their tier and fall through to the next scaling factor or the fallback logic. For example, a site with exactly 1000 previous emails will bypass the 1.25× tier and incorrectly use 1.5× scaling (getting 1500 instead of 1250). Similarly, 5000 will use 1.75× instead of 1.5×, and 100000 will use 2× instead of 1.75×. This creates unexpected jumps in scaling at precisely the documented threshold boundaries.

producedFindings.title: Filter condition change may include today's emails incorrectly
producedFindings.description: The filter condition was changed from `created_at:<` (strictly less than) to `created_at:<=` (less than or equal). The method comment states it should return 'The highest number of messages sent from the CSD in a single email (excluding today)'. With `<=`, emails created today at midnight (00:00:00) would be included, potentially violating the intended behavior of excluding today's emails.
producedFindings.recommendation: Revert to using strict less than (`<`) to ensure today's emails are properly excluded, or update the method documentation if the behavior change is intentional.

producedFindings.title: Test helper uses incorrect method name
producedFindings.description: The test helper `createModelClass` is being called with `findAll` property, but the actual service code uses `findPage` method. This suggests either the test helper is mapping `findAll` to `findPage` internally, or there's a naming inconsistency that could cause confusion for future maintainers.
producedFindings.recommendation: Align the test helper property name with the actual method name (`findPage`) for consistency and clarity, or document the mapping if it exists.

producedFindings.title: High volume threshold logic may produce unexpected results at boundary
producedFindings.description: When lastCount equals exactly 400,000, the high volume logic applies (returning min(480000, 475000) = 475000). However, the threshold table also has an entry for limit: 400,000 with scale: 2. The loop uses `lastCount < threshold.limit`, so at exactly 400,000 it would fall through to high volume handling. This is correct but the boundary behavior could be clearer.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider adding a comment clarifying the boundary behavior at exactly 400,000, or restructure the logic to make the precedence more explicit.

producedFindings.title: Filter operator change may incorrectly include today's emails
producedFindings.description: The filter changed from 'created_at:<' to 'created_at:<='. The comment states the method should return 'The highest number of messages sent from the CSD in a single email (excluding today)'. Using '<=' with today's date will include emails created at midnight today (00:00:00), which are technically 'today' and should be excluded according to the documented behavior.
producedFindings.recommendation: Either revert to using '<' operator, or if '<=' is intentional, update the comment to reflect that emails from midnight today are included. Consider using `created_at:<${today}` where today is the start of today.

producedFindings.title: Test assertion doesn't match implementation's filter format
producedFindings.description: The test asserts that the filter includes 'created_at:<' but the implementation now uses 'created_at:<='. This test will fail because the actual filter string is 'created_at:<=YYYY-MM-DD' which does not include the substring 'created_at:<YYYY-MM-DD' (note the missing '=' in the assertion).
producedFindings.recommendation: Update the test assertion to match the new filter format: `assert.ok(callArgs.filter.includes(`created_at:<=${today}`));`

producedFindings.title: Test mock uses findAll but implementation uses findPage
producedFindings.description: The test helper createModelClass is being called with 'findAll' property, but the actual DomainWarmingService implementation calls findPage with {data: EmailRecord[]} return type. This mismatch means the test mocks don't accurately represent the API the production code uses, which could lead to tests passing while production fails.
producedFindings.recommendation: Update the createModelClass helper and all test usages to use 'findPage' instead of 'findAll', returning {data: [...]} structure to match the actual API.

producedFindings.title: Integration test uses hardcoded expected values that may be fragile
producedFindings.description: The test asserts exact values (200, 250, 313) for warmup progression. While this tests the scaling correctly, the expected value 313 comes from Math.ceil(250 * 1.25) = 313. This is correct but makes the test harder to understand without the calculation context.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider adding a comment explaining the calculation: // Day 3: Math.ceil(250 * 1.25) = 313. This improves maintainability and makes it clear why 313 is expected.

producedFindings.title: Missing dependency in useEffect
producedFindings.description: The useEffect hook that checks if content is clamped has an empty dependency array, but it should re-run when the comment content (item.html) changes. If the comment content changes dynamically, the clamped state won't be recalculated.

The correct logic should require both the feature to be enabled AND the source to be in the allowed list.

groundTruth.description: The condition `hasScheduled.processOutbox && !process.env.NODE_ENV.startsWith('test')` is incorrect - it checks if the job is already scheduled before scheduling it, creating a logical impossibility. The job will never be scheduled on the first call since `hasScheduled.processOutbox` starts as `false`. This should be `!hasScheduled.processOutbox` to schedule the job when it hasn't been scheduled yet. This breaks the welcome email processing functionality entirely, as the background job that processes the outbox entries will never be registered.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect logical operator allows unwanted welcome email triggers
groundTruth.description: The condition uses OR (`||`) instead of AND (`&&`) when checking whether to create welcome email outbox entries. This means welcome emails will be triggered if EITHER the config is set OR the source is 'member', instead of requiring BOTH conditions. This causes two problems: (1) welcome emails are sent even when the feature is disabled (config not set) as long as source is 'member', and (2) welcome emails are sent for disallowed sources (like 'import' or 'admin') when the config is set. The correct logic should require both the feature to be enabled AND the source to be in the allowed list.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect require path for MagicLink module after file rename
groundTruth.description: The require statement references 'magic-link' (kebab-case) but the actual file is still named 'MagicLink.js' (PascalCase). This PR renamed many files to kebab-case, but this file was not renamed - it remains as MagicLink.js. When this code executes, Node.js will throw a MODULE_NOT_FOUND error because the path './../../lib/magic-link/magic-link' does not exist. The correct path should be './../../lib/magic-link/MagicLink' to match the actual file. This will cause the entire members API to fail to initialize, breaking member authentication, signup, and magic link functionality across the application.

producedFindings.title: Missing semicolons in getWarmupLimit method
producedFindings.description: Multiple statements are missing semicolons, which is inconsistent with TypeScript/JavaScript best practices and the rest of the codebase style.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add semicolons after each statement: `const day = await this.#getDaysSinceFirstEmail();` and `return Infinity;`

producedFindings.title: Missing semicolons in getWarmupLimit return statement
producedFindings.description: The return statement is missing a semicolon, inconsistent with standard TypeScript style.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add semicolon: `return Math.min(emailCount, limit);`

producedFindings.title: Test assertion uses hardcoded expected value that may not match formula
producedFindings.description: The test hardcodes the expected value (237) rather than computing it using the actual formula. The comment shows the formula, but if the implementation changes slightly (e.g., rounding method), the test would fail while the code might still be correct. The actual calculation 200 * Math.pow(1000, 1/41) ≈ 236.99, which floors to 236, not 237.
producedFindings.recommendation: Calculate the expected value using the same formula as the implementation: `const expectedLimit = Math.min(email2Count, Math.floor(200 * Math.pow(200000/200, 1/41)));`

producedFindings.title: Potential floating-point precision issue in warmup calculation
producedFindings.description: The calculation uses Math.floor, but the test at line 237 expects 237. However, 200 * Math.pow(1000, 1/41) ≈ 236.99, which would floor to 236. This discrepancy suggests either the test expectation is wrong or the implementation should use Math.round instead.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify the expected values match the formula. Consider using Math.round instead of Math.floor for more intuitive behavior, or update test expectations to match the actual computed values.

producedFindings.title: Day calculation uses Math.ceil which may cause off-by-one issues
producedFindings.snippet: return Math.ceil((Date.now() - new Date(res.data[0].get('created_at') as string).getTime()) / (1000 * 60 * 60 * 24));
producedFindings.description: Using Math.ceil for day calculation means an email sent 1 second ago returns day 1, not day 0. This could cause the warmup to progress faster than expected. For example, if the first email was sent today, the function returns 1 (not 0), causing the warmup to start at day 1's limit instead of day 0's.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using Math.floor instead of Math.ceil to ensure the first day is day 0, matching the test expectations and the documented behavior.

producedFindings.title: WarmupConfig is not configurable externally
producedFindings.description: The warmup configuration is hardcoded to use DefaultWarmupOptions with no way to customize it through the constructor or configuration. This reduces flexibility for different use cases or A/B testing different warmup strategies.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider allowing warmupConfig to be passed via the constructor dependencies or through the config service, falling back to DefaultWarmupOptions when not provided.

groundTruth.category: Code Must Always Use Semicolons
groundTruth.title: Missing semicolons in getWarmupLimit method
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all JavaScript and TypeScript statements to end with explicit semicolons, but this code relies on automatic semicolon insertion (ASI) instead

groundTruth.title: Incorrect rounding in day calculation causes off-by-one error in warmup schedule
groundTruth.description: The getDaysSinceFirstEmail method uses Math.ceil instead of Math.floor to calculate days since the first email. This causes the warmup to start one day ahead of schedule. For example, on the same day an email is sent (even minutes after), the partial day will round up to day 1 instead of day 0. This breaks the entire 42-day warmup progression as all subsequent calculations will be offset by one day, causing the service to use higher email limits than intended for each actual day, potentially harming domain reputation by warming up too aggressively.

groundTruth.title: Off-by-one error in warmup completion check extends warmup period
groundTruth.description: The condition checking if warmup is complete uses `day > this.#warmupConfig.totalDays` instead of `day >= this.#warmupConfig.totalDays`. With a 42-day warmup period (totalDays=42), this means the warmup won't complete until day 43 instead of day 42. On day 42, the system will still calculate and apply a limit instead of returning Infinity to indicate unlimited sending. This extends the warmup period by one full day, delaying when users can send emails at full volume and potentially causing confusion when the warmup doesn't complete as documented.

groundTruth.title: Using Math.floor instead of Math.round produces consistently lower warmup limits
groundTruth.description: The warmup limit calculation uses Math.floor instead of Math.round to compute the daily email limit. This systematically underestimates the intended limits throughout the warmup period. For exponential growth calculations, proper rounding is critical - when the formula produces values like 237.6, Math.floor gives 237 while Math.round would give 238. Over a 42-day warmup period, this accumulates significant deviation from the intended warmup curve, making the warmup more conservative than designed and potentially extending the effective time needed to reach full sending capacity. This undermines the carefully calculated exponential progression from 200 to 200,000 emails.

producedFindings.title: Day calculation returns wrong value for day 0
producedFindings.snippet: return Math.ceil((Date.now() - new Date(res.data[0].get('created_at') as string).getTime()) / (1000 * 60 * 60 * 24));
producedFindings.description: When the first email was sent today (same day), the time difference is 0, but `Math.ceil(0)` returns 0. However, for an email sent just milliseconds ago, the result would be 0, but the test expects day 0 to return the start value of 200. The issue is that `Math.ceil` on a small fractional day (e.g., 0.001 days = 86 seconds) would return 1, not 0. This means an email sent 2 minutes ago would be treated as day 1, not day 0.
producedFindings.recommendation: Use `Math.floor` instead of `Math.ceil` to properly calculate whole days elapsed, or clarify the semantics of what 'day 0' means (same calendar day vs. less than 24 hours).

producedFindings.title: Off-by-one error in exponential formula denominator
producedFindings.description: The formula uses `totalDays - 1` (41) as the denominator, meaning day 41 should reach the end value of 200000. However, the test case shows day 41 expects 200000, which works mathematically. But the warmup period check `day > this.#warmupConfig.totalDays` (day > 42) means day 42 returns Infinity. This creates an inconsistency: the warmup reaches full capacity on day 41, but isn't considered 'complete' until day 43.
producedFindings.recommendation: Align the warmup completion check with the formula. Either change the check to `day >= this.#warmupConfig.totalDays - 1` or adjust the formula to use `totalDays` as the denominator.

producedFindings.title: Returning Infinity may cause runtime errors in callers
producedFindings.description: After the warmup period, the service returns `Infinity` to indicate no limit. However, callers may not expect this value and could encounter issues when using it in arithmetic operations, JSON serialization, or database queries. The previous implementation always returned a finite number.
producedFindings.recommendation: Return a large finite number (e.g., `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` or the emailCount parameter) instead of `Infinity`, or document clearly that callers must handle `Infinity`.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect rounding in day calculation causes off-by-one error in warmup schedule
groundTruth.description: The getDaysSinceFirstEmail method uses Math.ceil instead of Math.floor to calculate days since the first email. This causes the warmup to start one day ahead of schedule. For example, on the same day an email is sent (even minutes after), the partial day will round up to day 1 instead of day 0. This breaks the entire 42-day warmup progression as all subsequent calculations will be offset by one day, causing the service to use higher email limits than intended for each actual day, potentially harming domain reputation by warming up too aggressively.

groundTruth.title: Off-by-one error in warmup completion check extends warmup period
groundTruth.description: The condition checking if warmup is complete uses `day > this.#warmupConfig.totalDays` instead of `day >= this.#warmupConfig.totalDays`. With a 42-day warmup period (totalDays=42), this means the warmup won't complete until day 43 instead of day 42. On day 42, the system will still calculate and apply a limit instead of returning Infinity to indicate unlimited sending. This extends the warmup period by one full day, delaying when users can send emails at full volume and potentially causing confusion when the warmup doesn't complete as documented.

groundTruth.title: Using Math.floor instead of Math.round produces consistently lower warmup limits
groundTruth.description: The warmup limit calculation uses Math.floor instead of Math.round to compute the daily email limit. This systematically underestimates the intended limits throughout the warmup period. For exponential growth calculations, proper rounding is critical - when the formula produces values like 237.6, Math.floor gives 237 while Math.round would give 238. Over a 42-day warmup period, this accumulates significant deviation from the intended warmup curve, making the warmup more conservative than designed and potentially extending the effective time needed to reach full sending capacity. This undermines the carefully calculated exponential progression from 200 to 200,000 emails.

groundTruth.title: Missing error handling in Bluesky enable function leaves UI in perpetual loading state
groundTruth.description: The handleEnable function removes the try-catch block that was wrapping the enableBlueskyMutation call. When the API call fails (network error, server error, etc.), the loading state is never reset to false, leaving the user stuck with a spinning loader and no way to retry. This is inconsistent with the handleDisable function which properly handles errors. The bug causes a poor user experience where users must refresh the page to recover from any API failure.

groundTruth.title: Polling logic performs one extra retry attempt beyond configured maximum
groundTruth.description: The confirmation polling interval increments the retry counter after checking if the limit is reached, combined with changing the condition from `>` to `>=`. This creates an off-by-one error where the system will actually make 13 API calls (retries 0-12) instead of the intended 12 calls defined by MAX_CONFIRMATION_RETRIES. The counter starts at 0, gets incremented to 12, makes a call, then on the next iteration checks if 12 >= 12 and only then stops. This wastes API resources and delays the timeout error message by one additional polling interval (5 seconds).

groundTruth.title: Missing query invalidation after Bluesky handle confirmation causes stale following list
groundTruth.description: The confirmBlueskyHandle mutation successfully updates the account cache but fails to invalidate the accountFollows query. When Bluesky is enabled via Bridgy Fed, the account automatically follows the brid.gy account. Both enableBluesky and disableBluesky mutations properly invalidate QUERY_KEYS.accountFollows('index', 'following') to refresh this list, but confirmBlueskyHandle does not. This means after the handle is confirmed, the UI won't show the brid.gy follow relationship until the user manually refreshes the page or navigates away and back. This is inconsistent with the established pattern in the codebase and creates a confusing user experience where the integration appears incomplete.

This bug affects user experience by showing jarring UI state changes during the transition.

groundTruth.title: Memory leak: temporary selected values not cleared on filter popover close

If the user then opens a different field's options, they may see incorrect pre-selected values carried over from the previous interaction. The original PR correctly added this cleanup in line 2102 to ensure temporary state is cleared, but this bug removes it causing state pollution across different filter field selections.

producedFindings.title: Empty addButtonText in StatsFilter regardless of filter state

producedFindings.title: Server token assigned object instead of token string
producedFindings.description: The code assigns `tokenData` (an object with `token` and `exp` properties) to `this._serverToken`, but previously it assigned `tokenData.token` (just the string). This will cause issues when the token is used elsewhere since consumers likely expect a string token value.
producedFindings.recommendation: Change `this._serverToken = tokenData;` back to `this._serverToken = tokenData.token;` to maintain the expected string value.

producedFindings.title: JWT verification replaced with decode, removing signature validation
producedFindings.description: The `_isJWTExpired` method now uses `jwt.decode(token)` instead of `jwt.verify(token, this.tinybirdConfig.adminToken)`. This removes cryptographic signature verification, meaning the function will accept tampered or forged tokens as valid. While this method only checks expiration, using unverified tokens could lead to security issues if the decoded payload is trusted elsewhere.
producedFindings.recommendation: Revert to using `jwt.verify(token, this.tinybirdConfig.adminToken)` to ensure the token's signature is validated before checking expiration. If you need to check expiration without throwing on invalid signatures, document why this is safe.

producedFindings.title: JWT noTimestamp option removed without explanation
producedFindings.description: The `noTimestamp: true` option was removed from `jwt.sign()`. This changes the token structure by adding an `iat` (issued at) claim. While this may be intentional, there's no explanation for the change, and it could affect token handling or caching behavior.
producedFindings.recommendation: If removing `noTimestamp` was intentional, consider adding a comment explaining why. If it was accidental, restore the option to maintain previous token behavior.

groundTruth.title: Documentation references npm instead of yarn

groundTruth.title: Token caching stores entire object instead of token string
groundTruth.description: In the getToken method, when caching the JWT token, the code now stores the entire tokenData object (containing both 'token' and 'exp' properties) instead of just extracting the token string. This causes the returned token structure to be malformed as {token: {token: string, exp: number}, exp: number} instead of the expected {token: string, exp: number}. This will break any consumers of the Tinybird API (like the /api/tinybird/token endpoint and stats/tinybird.js) that expect a string token value, causing authentication failures when making requests to Tinybird pipes.

groundTruth.title: JWT signature validation bypassed in token expiration check
groundTruth.description: The _isJWTExpired method now uses jwt.decode() instead of jwt.verify() to check token expiration. While jwt.verify() validates the token's signature and ensures it hasn't been tampered with, jwt.decode() only decodes the token without any cryptographic validation. This creates a security vulnerability where expired, tampered, or forged tokens could be accepted as valid. An attacker could potentially modify token claims (like extending expiration or changing site_uuid in scopes) and the system would accept the malicious token, bypassing authentication controls for Tinybird API access.

groundTruth.title: JWT token structure changed by removing noTimestamp option
groundTruth.description: The _generateToken method previously created JWT tokens with {noTimestamp: true} to exclude the 'iat' (issued at) claim from the token payload. This option has been removed, causing the JWT library to automatically include the 'iat' timestamp in the token. This changes the token structure that Tinybird expects and may cause authentication failures if Tinybird's API validates or expects a specific token payload structure without the 'iat' claim. The changed token format could also affect token caching behavior and invalidate existing token validation logic.

producedFindings.title: Inconsistent endpoint naming pattern between v1 and v2
producedFindings.description: The version suffix is prepended with underscore (e.g., 'api_kpis_v2'), but the comment says 'v2' -> 'api_kpis_v2' which is correct. producedFindings.title: Removed source filter handling without migration path

If the source filter is passed via options, it will now be processed through the generic Object.entries loop instead of being explicitly handled.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that the source filter still works correctly through the generic parameter handling, or restore explicit handling if needed for special cases like empty string handling.

producedFindings.description: The Tinybird pipe templates use Jinja2-style templating with parameters like {{ String(source) }}. While Tinybird's parameter handling provides some protection, the raw SQL templates could be vulnerable if parameter validation is insufficient. Parameters are passed directly from user input through the API.
producedFindings.recommendation: Ensure all parameters are properly typed and validated in the Tinybird pipe definitions. Consider adding explicit validation in the Node.js service layer before passing parameters to Tinybird.

producedFindings.title: Missing input validation for site_uuid parameter
producedFindings.description: The site_uuid parameter has a default value 'mock_site_uuid' which could potentially be used in production if not properly overridden. While this appears to be for testing purposes, the default could mask configuration errors.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider removing the default value or using a more obviously invalid default like 'INVALID_SITE_UUID' to make configuration errors more apparent.

producedFindings.title: Redundant code simplification in React component
producedFindings.description: The change from explicit children prop to passing children as a prop to FrameworkContext.Provider is functionally equivalent but uses a less common pattern. The original pattern with explicit children nesting is more idiomatic React.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider reverting to the original pattern for better readability, as the explicit children nesting is more familiar to React developers.

groundTruth.category: React Components Must Sort JSX Props in Standard Order
groundTruth.title: JSX props ordered incorrectly
groundTruth.description: The rule requires JSX props to be ordered with regular props before the children prop. In the FrameworkContext.Provider component, the children prop appears before the value prop, violating the standard ordering convention where regular props should come before children.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect version suffix order in endpoint URL construction
groundTruth.description: The frontend code constructs Tinybird endpoint URLs with the version prefix in reverse order compared to the backend. The frontend builds URLs as `v2_api_kpis` (version first) while the backend expects `api_kpis_v2` (version last). This mismatch causes the frontend to call non-existent endpoints when the version config is set, resulting in failed API calls and missing analytics data. The comment correctly describes the pattern as 'api_kpis_v2' but the implementation reverses this to `${config.version}_${endpointName}`.

groundTruth.title: Missing source parameter in Tinybird API request
groundTruth.description: The fetchRawTopContentData method removes the critical logic that passes the `source` filter parameter to the Tinybird API. While the method still handles `options.source` validation (including empty strings for 'Direct' traffic) at lines 105-108 in the comment, it no longer assigns it to `tinybirdOptions.source`. This means source-based filtering will silently fail—the API endpoint will be called without the source parameter, returning unfiltered results instead of data filtered by the requested traffic source.

This appears to be an incorrect change - Forward() would navigate forward in browser history, not back to the original page as the comment and assertion suggest.
producedFindings.recommendation: Revert this change back to Browser.Navigate().Back() to match the test's intent and comment, or update the comment if the test behavior was intentionally changed.

This change may be incorrect or the test may need to be reviewed for consistency.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify the intended behavior of this quarantined test. If the test is meant to verify NavigationException behavior, the switch should likely remain 'false'. Update the switch value or provide documentation explaining why the behavior was inverted.

producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that capturing the h1 element before navigation is intentional and correct. If the h1 element should be from the redirected page, the original order should be restored.

groundTruth.title: Stale element reference captured before navigation in test initialization
groundTruth.description: In the InitializeAsync method, _originalH1Element is captured by calling Browser.Exists(By.TagName("h1")) before navigating to the test page. This captures an element from whatever page the browser was previously on (from a prior test), then the Navigate() call loads the /redirect page. The stored element reference now points to a detached DOM node from the old page, making it stale. When test methods later access _originalH1Element.Text, they will encounter StaleElementReferenceException or get incorrect values because the element belongs to a different page's DOM that no longer exists in the browser.

groundTruth.title: Browser navigation uses Forward() instead of Back() breaking test flow
groundTruth.description: In the RedirectEnhancedGetToInternal test, after clicking a link that redirects from /redirect to /scroll-to-hash, the test attempts to verify that browser back navigation works correctly. However, the code calls Browser.Navigate().Forward() instead of Browser.Navigate().Back(). Since there is no forward history (the browser just navigated forward to the current page), Forward() does nothing and the browser remains at /scroll-to-hash. The subsequent assertions expect the browser to be back at /redirect with the "Redirections" heading, but it's still at the redirected page, causing the test to fail. The comment explicitly states "See that 'back' takes you to the place from before the redirection" but the code does the opposite action.

groundTruth.title: Navigation exception test disables the exception mechanism it's meant to test
groundTruth.description: The NavigationException_InAsyncContext_DoesNotBecomeUnobservedTaskException test is designed to verify that NavigationException thrown during circular redirects doesn't become an unobserved task exception. The test name, comments, and logic all indicate it should test the exception handling behavior. However, the AppContext switch is set to true, which enables DisableThrowNavigationException, preventing NavigationException from being thrown at all. This fundamentally breaks the test's purpose - it can't validate exception handling behavior when exceptions are disabled. The test navigates to a circular redirect page that relies on NavigationException being thrown and properly handled, but with the switch enabled, the navigation manager uses a different code path that doesn't throw exceptions, making the test validate nothing meaningful.

groundTruth.description: The rule requires using xUnit test framework attributes. The added [QuarantinedTest] attribute is not an xUnit attribute ([Fact]/[Theory]) and introduces non-xUnit test metadata on the test method.

producedFindings.title: Incorrect navigation direction in test assertion
producedFindings.description: The comment above this line states "See that 'back' takes you to the place from before the redirection" but the code now calls Forward() instead of Back(). This is logically incorrect - to test that 'back' navigation works correctly, the test should call Back(), not Forward().
producedFindings.recommendation: Revert this change back to Browser.Navigate().Back() to match the test's intent and comment, or update the comment if the test behavior was intentionally changed.

producedFindings.title: Test logic changed in quarantined test
producedFindings.description: The test 'NavigationException_InAsyncContext_DoesNotBecomeUnobservedTaskException' was quarantined and the DisableThrowNavigationException switch was changed from 'false' to 'true'. This fundamentally changes what the test is validating - it was testing behavior when navigation exceptions are thrown, but now tests behavior when they are disabled. This appears to be making the test pass by changing its purpose rather than fixing the underlying issue.
producedFindings.recommendation: Investigate why the test was failing with the original switch value. If the test needs to be quarantined, keep the original test logic intact so it can be properly fixed later. Changing the test logic defeats the purpose of quarantining.

producedFindings.title: Test initialization order changed
producedFindings.description: The order of operations in InitializeAsync was changed: previously Navigate() was called first, then _originalH1Element was set. Now _originalH1Element is set before Navigate(). This could cause the element to be captured from the wrong page state.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify this change is intentional and the element capture works correctly before navigation. If the original order was correct, revert this change.

producedFindings.title: Mixed test framework attributes
producedFindings.description: A MSTest attribute [TestMethod] was added alongside the existing xUnit [Fact] attribute usage. The file now mixes xUnit and MSTest attributes, which could cause confusion about which test framework is being used.
producedFindings.recommendation: Use consistent test framework attributes throughout the file. If migrating to MSTest, update all tests consistently. If using QuarantinedTest attribute requires MSTest, consider whether this is the right approach.

groundTruth.category: Test Projects Must Use xUnit Framework
groundTruth.title: MSTest attribute in xUnit project
groundTruth.description: The rule requires test projects to consistently use the xUnit framework, but the test method uses MSTest's [TestMethod] attribute instead of xUnit's [Fact] attribute

groundTruth.title: Stale element reference captured before navigation in test initialization
groundTruth.description: In the InitializeAsync method, _originalH1Element is captured by calling Browser.Exists(By.TagName("h1")) before navigating to the test page. This captures an element from whatever page the browser was previously on (from a prior test), then the Navigate() call loads the /redirect page. The stored element reference now points to a detached DOM node from the old page, making it stale. When test methods later access _originalH1Element.Text, they will encounter StaleElementReferenceException or get incorrect values because the element belongs to a different page's DOM that no longer exists in the browser.

groundTruth.title: Browser navigation uses Forward() instead of Back() breaking test flow
groundTruth.description: In the RedirectEnhancedGetToInternal test, after clicking a link that redirects from /redirect to /scroll-to-hash, the test attempts to verify that browser back navigation works correctly. However, the code calls Browser.Navigate().Forward() instead of Browser.Navigate().Back(). Since there is no forward history (the browser just navigated forward to the current page), Forward() does nothing and the browser remains at /scroll-to-hash. The subsequent assertions expect the browser to be back at /redirect with the "Redirections" heading, but it's still at the redirected page, causing the test to fail. The comment explicitly states "See that 'back' takes you to the place from before the redirection" but the code does the opposite action.

groundTruth.title: Navigation exception test disables the exception mechanism it's meant to test
groundTruth.description: The NavigationException_InAsyncContext_DoesNotBecomeUnobservedTaskException test is designed to verify that NavigationException thrown during circular redirects doesn't become an unobserved task exception. The test name, comments, and logic all indicate it should test the exception handling behavior. However, the AppContext switch is set to true, which enables DisableThrowNavigationException, preventing NavigationException from being thrown at all. This fundamentally breaks the test's purpose - it can't validate exception handling behavior when exceptions are disabled. The test navigates to a circular redirect page that relies on NavigationException being thrown and properly handled, but with the switch enabled, the navigation manager uses a different code path that doesn't throw exceptions, making the test validate nothing meaningful.

groundTruth.category: Test Projects Must Use xUnit Framework
groundTruth.title: MSTest dependency imported in xUnit test project
groundTruth.description: The repository rule requires test projects to use xUnit. Adding an MSTest namespace import indicates use of an alternative test framework (MSTest) in this test file.

groundTruth.category: Test Projects Must Use xUnit Framework
groundTruth.title: Non-xUnit test attribute added
groundTruth.description: The rule requires using xUnit test framework attributes. The added [QuarantinedTest] attribute is not an xUnit attribute ([Fact]/[Theory]) and introduces non-xUnit test metadata on the test method.

producedFindings.title: Assert logic requires both regex patterns to match simultaneously
producedFindings.description: The test assertion uses AND logic (&&) between two regex patterns, meaning both patterns must match for the test to pass. However, the error message format likely changed between browser versions, so only one pattern should match at a time. The assertion message suggests the intent is 'match one of the patterns' but the logic requires both.
producedFindings.recommendation: Change the AND operator (&&) to OR operator (||) to match either the old or new error message format: `ParseErrorMessageRegexOld.IsMatch(logs[0].Message) || ParseErrorMessageRegexNew.IsMatch(logs[0].Message)`

producedFindings.title: Test disposal order change may affect test reliability
producedFindings.description: The order of disposing connections was swapped - previously 'connection' was disposed before 'secondConnection', now 'secondConnection' is disposed first. While this may be intentional, the change is not documented and could affect test behavior if there are dependencies between the connections during disposal.
producedFindings.recommendation: If this change is intentional to fix a test reliability issue, consider adding a comment explaining why the disposal order matters. If unintentional, revert to the original order.

groundTruth.category: Sealed Keyword Required for Internal Implementation Classes
groundTruth.title: Internal class missing sealed keyword
groundTruth.description: Internal implementation classes that are not designed for inheritance must be marked with the 'sealed' keyword. The WebSocketWrapper class is an internal implementation class that should be sealed to prevent unintended subclassing.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect boolean operator in regex validation
groundTruth.description: The test assertion uses AND (&&) operator instead of OR (||) when checking if the log message matches either the old or new CSP error pattern. This causes the test to fail because a log message cannot simultaneously match both regex patterns - they represent different browser error message formats. The original intent was to accept either format, but the AND operator requires both patterns to match at the same time, which is impossible. This will cause the test to fail even when receiving a valid CSP error message in either format.

groundTruth.title: Disposal order causing potential race condition
groundTruth.description: The connection disposal order is reversed - disposing secondConnection before the connection that is still being used. In the test, 'connection' performs an InvokeAsync call and its TaskCompletionSource (tcs) is awaited immediately before disposal. However, disposing secondConnection first may cause premature cleanup of shared resources or message handlers since both connections share the same user ('*'). This can lead to flaky test behavior where the connection's pending operations are interrupted by the disposal of secondConnection, especially in distributed scenarios where both connections interact with the same Redis backplane.

groundTruth.title: Race condition from premature TaskCompletionSource recreation
groundTruth.description: The _receiveTcs TaskCompletionSource is recreated before checking if the buffer is zero-sized, creating a race condition in the WebSocketWrapper's ReceiveAsync implementation. When a zero-byte read occurs (buffer.Count == 0), the method returns early with the result but the TCS has already been reset. This means if another thread or the background task tries to set a result on the now-recreated TCS while this zero-byte read is in progress, it will affect the next receive operation rather than completing the current one. This breaks the synchronization contract and can cause message loss or out-of-order receives in the stateful reconnection test scenarios where WebSocket message sequencing is critical.

producedFindings.title: Shell variable expansion bug in Event 114 message
producedFindings.description: The message template uses `${1}` which would expand to the value of the environment variable name (e.g., if envVarName is 'SSL_CERT_DIR', it would produce `${SSL_CERT_DIR}`). However, this is inside a backtick-quoted command example, and the `$` needs proper escaping or the example won't work as intended when displayed to users. The intent seems to be showing how to append to an existing variable, but the syntax is confusing.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using a clearer example like `export {1}="{0}:$${1}"` to properly escape the dollar sign, or provide a concrete example that doesn't rely on shell variable expansion in the message template.

producedFindings.title: Event listener not disposed
producedFindings.description: The ReporterEventListener is created and enabled but never disposed. EventListener implements IDisposable and should be disposed to properly release resources and unregister from event sources.
producedFindings.recommendation: Wrap the listener creation in a using statement or try/finally block to ensure proper disposal.

producedFindings.title: Unnecessary refactoring of NssDb from positional record to verbose class
producedFindings.description: The NssDb class was refactored from a concise positional record (primary constructor syntax) to a verbose traditional class with explicit backing fields. This adds boilerplate without any apparent benefit - the original record syntax was more concise and equally functional for this simple immutable data container.
producedFindings.recommendation: Revert to the original positional record syntax which was more concise and idiomatic for simple immutable data containers.

producedFindings.title: Path comparison may fail for paths with trailing slashes
producedFindings.description: Path.GetFullPath may normalize paths differently depending on trailing slashes or symbolic links. On Unix systems, two paths that refer to the same directory might not compare equal if one has a trailing slash and the other doesn't, though Path.GetFullPath typically handles this. Additionally, symbolic links could cause the same physical directory to have different resolved paths.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using Directory.Exists and comparing canonical paths, or add a trailing slash normalization before comparison to handle edge cases more robustly.

groundTruth.category: Use Primary Constructor Syntax Where Appropriate
groundTruth.title: Traditional constructor instead of primary
groundTruth.description: The NssDb class uses traditional constructor syntax with repetitive parameter-to-field assignments when primary constructor syntax would be more concise

groundTruth.title: Case-insensitive path comparison on Unix breaks certificate directory detection
groundTruth.description: The SSL_CERT_DIR validation logic uses StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase when comparing Unix file paths. On Unix systems, file paths are case-sensitive, so '/home/user/certs' and '/home/user/Certs' are different directories. This case-insensitive comparison will incorrectly match different directories, causing the tool to report that the certificate directory is already configured when it's not, preventing proper certificate trust setup and leaving certificates untrusted.

groundTruth.title: Using display path instead of actual path breaks certificate directory validation
groundTruth.description: The code uses prettyCertDir (which contains '$HOME' placeholder for display purposes) instead of certDir (the actual file system path) when computing certDirFullPath. Since prettyCertDir contains the literal string '$HOME' rather than the expanded home directory path, Path.GetFullPath() will fail to resolve it correctly or resolve it to an incorrect path. This causes the directory comparison logic to always fail, preventing the tool from detecting when the certificate directory is already configured in SSL_CERT_DIR, resulting in unnecessary warning messages to users who have already configured their environment correctly.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect event level causes important certificate warnings to be suppressed in non-verbose mode
groundTruth.description: When verbose mode is not enabled, the event listener is configured with EventLevel.LogAlways instead of the intended EventLevel.Critical. EventLevel.LogAlways (value 0) actually means all events are enabled, not just critical ones. The original PR intended to show only Critical events in non-verbose mode to suppress routine logging. This bug causes all certificate-related events including verbose debug messages to be displayed in non-verbose mode, flooding users with unnecessary output and making it harder to identify actual problems. This breaks the intended user experience where only critical information should be shown by default.

groundTruth.category: Opening Braces Must Be on New Line (Allman Style)
groundTruth.title: Lambda block uses K&R-style opening brace
groundTruth.description: The opening brace for the lambda block is on the same line as the lambda declaration (`dir => {`). Rule 18 requires Allman style (opening braces on a new line) for blocks.

producedFindings.title: Path comparison uses wrong variable for full path
producedFindings.description: The code compares paths to check if certDir is already in SSL_CERT_DIR, but it computes the full path from 'prettyCertDir' (which may contain '$HOME' prefix for display purposes) instead of the actual 'certDir' path. This could lead to incorrect comparison results when the pretty path contains '$HOME' which Path.GetFullPath won't expand properly.
producedFindings.recommendation: Use 'certDir' instead of 'prettyCertDir' when computing certDirFullPath, since certDir contains the actual expanded path. The prettyCertDir is meant for display/logging purposes only.

producedFindings.title: EventListener never disposed
producedFindings.description: The ReporterEventListener is created and enabled but never disposed. EventListener instances should be disposed to properly clean up event subscriptions and prevent resource leaks, especially in long-running processes.
producedFindings.recommendation: Wrap the listener in a using statement or call Dispose() when appropriate, e.g., 'using var listener = new ReporterEventListener(reporter);'

producedFindings.title: Redundant variable initialization
producedFindings.description: The variable hasValidSslCertDir is initialized to false and then explicitly set to false again in the else block. This is redundant since the variable is already false at that point.
producedFindings.recommendation: Remove the redundant 'hasValidSslCertDir = false;' assignment in the else block since the variable is already initialized to false.

producedFindings.title: Incorrect format placeholder in event message
producedFindings.snippet:
[Event(114, Level = EventLevel.LogAlways, Message = "For OpenSSL trust to take effect, '{0}' must be listed in the {1} environment variable. " +
            "For example, `export {1}=\"{0}:${1}\"`. " +
            "See https://aka.ms/dev-certs-trust for more information.")]
        internal void UnixSuggestAppendingToEnvironmentVariable(string certDir, string envVarName) => WriteEvent(114, certDir, envVarName);
producedFindings.description: The message format string uses `${1}` which will expand to the environment variable name (e.g., 'SSL_CERT_DIR'), not the current value of that variable. The intent appears to be to show how to append to the existing variable value, but the placeholder syntax is wrong. The result would be something like `export SSL_CERT_DIR="/path/to/cert:$SSL_CERT_DIR"` which is actually correct shell syntax, but the message format is confusing because it uses EventSource placeholders mixed with shell variable syntax.
producedFindings.recommendation: The message appears to be intentionally using shell variable syntax (${1} becomes $SSL_CERT_DIR in the output). If this is intentional, consider adding a code comment to clarify. If the intent was to show the actual current value, pass it as a third parameter and use {2} in the format string.

producedFindings.title: EventListener may not be properly disposed
producedFindings.description: The ReporterEventListener is created and enabled for all code paths now (previously only in verbose mode), but there's no visible disposal pattern. EventListener implements IDisposable and should be disposed to properly clean up event subscriptions. Without seeing the full context, it's unclear if this listener is disposed elsewhere.
producedFindings.recommendation: Ensure the ReporterEventListener is wrapped in a using statement or otherwise properly disposed when no longer needed to prevent resource leaks.

producedFindings.title: Removed XML documentation comment
producedFindings.description: The PR removes a remarks comment that documented important behavior about --verbose flag being required for message display. This information was useful for developers understanding the event source behavior.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider restoring the documentation comment or moving it to a more appropriate location if it no longer applies.

groundTruth.description: When verbose mode is not enabled, the event listener is configured with EventLevel.LogAlways instead of the intended EventLevel.Critical. EventLevel.LogAlways (value 0) actually means all events are enabled, not just critical ones. The original PR intended to show only Critical events in non-verbose mode to suppress routine logging. This bug causes all certificate-related events including verbose debug messages to be displayed in non-verbose mode, flooding users with unnecessary output and making it harder to identify actual problems. This breaks the intended user experience where only critical information should be shown by default.

groundTruth.category: Opening Braces Must Be on New Line (Allman Style)
groundTruth.title: Lambda block uses K&R-style opening brace
groundTruth.description: The opening brace for the lambda block is on the same line as the lambda declaration (`dir => {`). Rule 18 requires Allman style (opening braces on a new line) for blocks.

producedFindings.title: Display name cache not cleared on hot reload
producedFindings.description: The ClearCache method only clears _memberInfoCache but not _displayNameCache. During hot reload, if a DisplayAttribute or DisplayNameAttribute is modified on a property, the stale display name will still be returned from the cache.
producedFindings.recommendation: Also clear _displayNameCache in the ClearCache method: _displayNameCache.Clear();

producedFindings.title: Expression comparison uses reference equality
producedFindings.description: The comparison uses the != operator on Expression<Func<TValue>> which performs reference equality. If a parent component re-renders and passes a new but semantically equivalent expression instance, the display name will be recalculated unnecessarily. This is a minor performance concern, not a correctness issue.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using ExpressionEqualityComparer from System.Linq.Expressions or comparing expression strings if performance optimization is needed. Alternatively, document that this is intentional behavior for simplicity.

groundTruth.title: Unnecessary re-rendering on every parameter update with same display name
groundTruth.description: The DisplayName component removed the check that prevents re-rendering when the display name hasn't changed (line 50-51). This means that every time SetParametersAsync is called with a changed expression (even if it results in the same display name), the component will trigger a render. This causes performance degradation in scenarios with frequent parameter updates, such as forms with dynamic field expressions, leading to unnecessary DOM updates and potential flickering in the UI.

groundTruth.title: Missing display name caching causes performance degradation
groundTruth.description: The GetDisplayName(MemberInfo) method in ExpressionMemberAccessor no longer uses the _displayNameCache dictionary to cache display names (lines 53-74). Instead, it performs reflection operations (GetCustomAttribute) on every call. Since this method is called frequently during component rendering and the DisplayName component can be used extensively throughout forms, this creates a significant performance bottleneck. The cache was specifically designed to avoid repeated reflection calls, and its removal will cause measurable slowdowns in forms with many DisplayName components.

groundTruth.title: Incomplete cache clearing during hot reload causes stale display names
groundTruth.description: The ClearCache method only clears _memberInfoCache but fails to clear _displayNameCache (line 85). During hot reload scenarios when metadata updates occur, the HotReloadManager triggers ClearCache to ensure fresh metadata is loaded. However, with _displayNameCache not being cleared, the component will continue to display stale display names from cached MemberInfo objects even after the attributes have been modified in code. This breaks the hot reload functionality for DisplayAttribute and DisplayNameAttribute changes, requiring developers to restart the application to see attribute changes take effect.

producedFindings.title: Expression cache may cause memory leaks with closures
producedFindings.description: The _memberInfoCache uses Expression as the key with reference equality. When lambda expressions capture variables (closures), each expression instance is unique and will never be garbage collected, potentially causing memory leaks in applications that frequently create such expressions.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using a structural comparison for expressions, or implement a cache eviction policy (e.g., LRU cache with size limit). Alternatively, document that this cache is intended for hot paths and that typical usage patterns should reuse expression instances.

producedFindings.title: Display name cache not cleared on hot reload
producedFindings.description: The ClearCache method only clears _memberInfoCache but not _displayNameCache. During hot reload, display names from attributes could become stale if attributes are modified.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add `_displayNameCache.Clear();` to the ClearCache method to ensure both caches are cleared during hot reload.

producedFindings.title: Inconsistent indentation in Login.razor
producedFindings.description: The password input field and its surrounding elements have inconsistent indentation compared to the email field above it, breaking the code style consistency.
producedFindings.recommendation: Fix the indentation of the password input field div and its children to match the surrounding code structure (add proper leading spaces).

producedFindings.title: Missing Display attributes on ChangePassword and Register input models
producedFindings.description: The ChangePassword.razor and Register.razor templates use DisplayName component for OldPassword, NewPassword, ConfirmPassword fields, but the corresponding Input model properties don't have [Display] attributes. This means the DisplayName component will fall back to property names (e.g., 'OldPassword' instead of 'Old password').
producedFindings.recommendation: Add [Display(Name = "Old password")], [Display(Name = "New password")], and [Display(Name = "Confirm password")] attributes to the corresponding properties in the Input models in ChangePassword.razor and Register.razor.

groundTruth.category: Use File-Scoped Namespace Declarations

groundTruth.title: Incorrect type conversion in generic component parameter assertion
groundTruth.description: The test validates a generic component with an int type parameter but uses Convert.ToInt64 instead of Convert.ToInt32 to assert the parameter value. Since the parameter is defined as int (System.Int32) in GenericTestComponent<int>, this creates a type mismatch. The assertion compares a long (Int64) value to the expected int (Int32) value 42, which will cause the test to fail even though the component deserialization is working correctly. This breaks the validation logic for generic component parameters.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect sequence validation for multiple generic components
groundTruth.description: The test validates deserialization of multiple closed generic components but checks that both descriptors have sequence number 0 instead of verifying that the second descriptor has sequence number 1. Component descriptors in a collection must have sequential ordering starting from 0, and this test should verify the second component has sequence 1. This incorrect assertion fails to validate proper sequence ordering, which is critical for component initialization and rendering order in Blazor.

groundTruth.title: Generic type parameter mismatch in component marker validation
groundTruth.description: The test creates and renders a GenericComponent<int> with value 456, but then validates the component marker against GenericComponent<string> type instead of GenericComponent<int>. This causes the test to check for the wrong fully qualified type name and assembly metadata. The type validation will fail because the actual rendered component is GenericComponent<int> while the test expects GenericComponent<string>, breaking the validation of WebAssembly component prerendering for closed generic types.
