producedFindings.title: Potential null reference in Redirect fallback path
producedFindings.description: In the else branch, when InteractionOptions is null, the code now navigates to InteractiveRequestUrl instead of the removed RedirectUrl. However, if both InteractionOptions and InteractiveRequestUrl are null, this will pass null to NavigateTo, potentially causing a null argument exception. The previous code used RedirectUrl which was guaranteed to have a value in this context based on the old constructor.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add a null check for InteractiveRequestUrl before navigating, or throw an InvalidOperationException if both InteractionOptions and InteractiveRequestUrl are null to provide a clearer error message.

producedFindings.title: Removed SignOutSessionStateManager breaks backward compatibility
producedFindings.description: The SignOutSessionStateManager class is completely deleted. While it was marked obsolete, any external code still using this class will experience a compilation break. This is a breaking change for consumers who haven't migrated to the recommended NavigationManagerExtensions.NavigateToLogout approach.
producedFindings.recommendation: This is intentional based on the obsolete message. Ensure release notes clearly document this breaking change and provide migration guidance for users still relying on SignOutSessionStateManager.

groundTruth.category: Async Methods Must Use ConfigureAwait(false) in Library Code
groundTruth.title: Missing ConfigureAwait(false) on Task.Yield
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all await expressions in library code (non-test, non-sample projects) to use .ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid capturing the synchronization context, but the await Task.Yield() expression at line 291 omits it.

groundTruth.title: Logout validation incorrectly rejects valid logout requests when HistoryEntryState is null
groundTruth.description: The sign-out validation logic was modified to remove the null check for Navigation.HistoryEntryState before calling ValidateSignOutRequestState(). This causes ValidateSignOutRequestState() to return false when HistoryEntryState is null (since GetCachedNavigationState() returns null), which incorrectly redirects users to the logout failure page even for legitimate logout operations. The original code correctly allowed logout when HistoryEntryState is null by using the condition 'HistoryEntryState != null && !ValidateSignOutRequestState()', which only validated if state existed. This breaks backward compatibility with logout flows that don't use history state.

groundTruth.title: Missing null check for InteractiveRequestUrl causes potential null reference in navigation
groundTruth.description: The Redirect() method condition was changed from checking both InteractionOptions and InteractiveRequestUrl to only checking InteractionOptions. This creates a bug where if InteractionOptions is not null but InteractiveRequestUrl is null, the code will call NavigateToLogin with a null URL parameter at line 40. The NavigateToLogin method expects a non-null URL, so this will cause a null reference exception or navigation failure. The original PR properly validated both properties before choosing the navigation method.

producedFindings.title: Potential null reference in Redirect fallback path
producedFindings.description: In the else branch, the code calls _navigation.NavigateTo(_tokenResult.InteractiveRequestUrl!) but the condition only checks if _tokenResult.InteractionOptions != null. If InteractionOptions is null but InteractiveRequestUrl is also null, this will pass null to NavigateTo (after suppressing the null warning with !). The previous code used RedirectUrl in the fallback, but RedirectUrl has been removed. The new code assumes InteractiveRequestUrl is always available in the fallback case, which may not be true.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that InteractiveRequestUrl is guaranteed to be non-null when InteractionOptions is null, or add appropriate null checking/handling for this case.

producedFindings.title: Added Task.Yield before authentication check without clear justification
producedFindings.description: A call to await Task.Yield() was added before getting the authentication state. The comment says 'Ensure we're authenticated before proceeding with sign-out' but Task.Yield doesn't ensure authentication - it just yields control back to the scheduler. This appears to be a workaround for some timing issue but lacks proper documentation of why it's needed.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add a more detailed comment explaining why Task.Yield is necessary here, or consider if there's a more explicit way to achieve the intended behavior.

groundTruth.category: Async Methods Must Use ConfigureAwait(false) in Library Code
groundTruth.title: Missing ConfigureAwait(false) on Task.Yield
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all await expressions in library code (non-test, non-sample projects) to use .ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid capturing the synchronization context, but the await Task.Yield() expression at line 291 omits it.

groundTruth.title: Logout validation incorrectly rejects valid logout requests when HistoryEntryState is null
groundTruth.description: The sign-out validation logic was modified to remove the null check for Navigation.HistoryEntryState before calling ValidateSignOutRequestState(). This causes ValidateSignOutRequestState() to return false when HistoryEntryState is null (since GetCachedNavigationState() returns null), which incorrectly redirects users to the logout failure page even for legitimate logout operations. The original code correctly allowed logout when HistoryEntryState is null by using the condition 'HistoryEntryState != null && !ValidateSignOutRequestState()', which only validated if state existed. This breaks backward compatibility with logout flows that don't use history state.

groundTruth.title: Missing null check for InteractiveRequestUrl causes potential null reference in navigation
groundTruth.description: The Redirect() method condition was changed from checking both InteractionOptions and InteractiveRequestUrl to only checking InteractionOptions. This creates a bug where if InteractionOptions is not null but InteractiveRequestUrl is null, the code will call NavigateToLogin with a null URL parameter at line 40. The NavigateToLogin method expects a non-null URL, so this will cause a null reference exception or navigation failure. The original PR properly validated both properties before choosing the navigation method.

producedFindings.title: Potential null reference in AccessTokenNotAvailableException.Redirect
producedFindings.description: The else branch now calls _navigation.NavigateTo(_tokenResult.InteractiveRequestUrl!) with a null-forgiving operator. Previously it used _tokenResult.RedirectUrl which was guaranteed to be set by the obsolete constructor. With the obsolete constructor removed, if InteractiveRequestUrl is null, this will cause a null reference exception or navigate to an invalid URL.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that InteractiveRequestUrl is always set when InteractionOptions is null, or add proper null handling. Consider throwing an InvalidOperationException if both InteractionOptions and InteractiveRequestUrl are null.

producedFindings.title: Added Task.Yield without clear justification
producedFindings.description: A call to await Task.Yield() was added before GetAuthenticationStateAsync() with the comment 'Ensure we're authenticated before proceeding with sign-out'. This pattern is unusual and the comment doesn't explain why yielding is necessary.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add a more detailed comment explaining why Task.Yield is needed here, or remove it if it was added for debugging purposes. If there's a timing issue with authentication state, consider a more explicit synchronization approach.

producedFindings.title: Breaking change: Removed SignOutSessionStateManager without migration guide
producedFindings.description: SignOutSessionStateManager is completely deleted. While it was marked obsolete, the PR description doesn't mention migration guidance for users who may still be using this class.
producedFindings.recommendation: Ensure the PR description or release notes include clear migration guidance for users transitioning from SignOutSessionStateManager to the recommended alternative (NavigationManagerExtensions.NavigateToLogout).

producedFindings.title: JS component initializers not called when renderer reconnects
producedFindings.description: When a circuit restarts and enableJSRootComponents is called again with the same rendererId, the hasInitializedJsComponents flag prevents the JS component initializers from being called again. However, the jsComponentParametersByIdentifier is also not updated, which could lead to stale parameter definitions if they differ between circuit instances.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider whether jsComponentParametersByIdentifier should be updated even when hasInitializedJsComponents is true, or document clearly why this is safe. If the parameters are guaranteed to be the same across circuit restarts, add a comment explaining this assumption.

producedFindings.title: Missing braces on for loop body
producedFindings.description: The inner for loop is missing braces around its body, which is inconsistent with the coding style used elsewhere in the file and the original code. This was likely an unintentional change during refactoring.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add braces around the for loop body for consistency: `for (const componentIdentifier of componentIdentifiers) { initializerFunc(componentIdentifier, jsComponentParameters[componentIdentifier]); }`

producedFindings.title: Error message comment mismatch
producedFindings.description: The comment says 'A different renderer type (e.g., Server vs WebAssembly) is trying to enable JS root components' but the condition `manager && currentRendererId === rendererId` actually throws when the SAME renderer tries to re-enable (when manager exists and rendererId matches). The comment describes the opposite scenario.
producedFindings.recommendation: Correct the comment to accurately reflect the condition: 'The same renderer is trying to re-enable JS root components when already enabled. This can happen in multi-host scenarios which are not supported.'

groundTruth.category: Use Curly Braces for All Control Flow Statements
groundTruth.title: Missing braces in for loop
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all control flow statements (if, else, for, foreach, while, do-while) to use curly braces even for single-line bodies, but the inner for loop omits curly braces

groundTruth.title: Inverted renderer type check prevents circuit restart
groundTruth.description: The condition checking for different renderer types has been inverted from `!==` to `===`. This causes the function to throw an error when the SAME renderer type tries to re-enable JS root components (e.g., during circuit restart), which is the exact scenario that should be allowed. The original intent was to throw an error only when a DIFFERENT renderer type attempts to enable root components. This bug breaks circuit restart functionality, causing applications to fail when users reconnect after a circuit disconnect.

groundTruth.title: Missing parameter update causes stale data on circuit restart
groundTruth.description: The assignment `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier = jsComponentParameters;` has been removed from the re-enabling logic. When a circuit restarts and `enableJSRootComponents` is called again with new parameters, the global `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` is not updated. This causes the application to continue using stale parameters from the previous circuit. The `DynamicRootComponent` class constructor and methods rely on `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` to retrieve component parameter definitions, so they will operate with outdated metadata after a circuit restart.

groundTruth.title: Test method missing Arrange/Act/Assert comments
groundTruth.description: The added test method does not contain explicit '// Arrange', '// Act', and '// Assert' (or '// Act & Assert') comments to delineate the AAA phases, violating the required test structure convention.

producedFindings.title: Missing braces in for loop body
producedFindings.description: The for loop body is missing braces, which is inconsistent with the coding style used elsewhere in the file (e.g., the outer for-in loop has braces). This can lead to maintenance issues and potential bugs if additional statements are added later.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add braces around the loop body for consistency and safety: `for (const componentIdentifier of componentIdentifiers) { initializerFunc(componentIdentifier, jsComponentParameters[componentIdentifier]); }`

producedFindings.title: jsComponentParametersByIdentifier not updated on re-initialization
producedFindings.description: The variable `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` is no longer assigned when `hasInitializedJsComponents` is true. If the component parameters change between circuit restarts, the old parameters would still be used. However, since the initializers are only called once and they receive the parameters directly, this may be intentional - but the variable remains uninitialized on subsequent calls.
producedFindings.recommendation: Either move the `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier = jsComponentParameters;` assignment outside the `if (!hasInitializedJsComponents)` block, or document why the parameters don't need to be updated on re-initialization. If the variable is unused elsewhere, consider removing it.

producedFindings.title: Potential memory leak with old manager reference
producedFindings.description: When a circuit restarts, the old manager reference is simply overwritten without disposal. The comment explains that disposing would cause JSDisconnectedException, which is correct. However, the old DotNetObjectReference may hold resources until garbage collection. This appears to be an intentional trade-off documented in the code.
producedFindings.recommendation: The current approach is acceptable given the constraints. Consider adding a comment noting that the old manager will be garbage collected eventually, or track if this causes issues in practice.

producedFindings.title: Potential stale jsComponentParameters reference after re-initialization
producedFindings.description: When the same renderer re-enables JS root components (circuit restart), the code updates `manager` and `currentRendererId` but does NOT update `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier`. The `hasInitializedJsComponents` guard prevents re-running initializers, but if the parameters for any component changed between circuits, the stored parameters would be stale. The initializers are only called once with the first set of parameters.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider whether `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` should also be updated on re-initialization, or document clearly that component parameters are fixed after first initialization. If parameters can differ between circuits, either update the stored parameters or pass them fresh to initializers.

producedFindings.title: Old manager reference not explicitly released
producedFindings.description: When a new manager is assigned during circuit restart, the old manager reference is simply overwritten. While the comment correctly notes that disposing would cause JSDisconnectedException, there's no explicit cleanup or nulling of the old reference before assignment. This is a minor concern as the old DotNetObjectReference is no longer valid anyway.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider adding a comment or explicit assignment to clarify intent: `// Old manager is orphaned - its DotNetObjectReference is invalid because the old circuit is gone`

producedFindings.title: Missing type annotations for function parameters
producedFindings.description: The function parameter `items` has no type annotation in a TypeScript file. This defeats TypeScript's purpose of catching type errors at compile time and makes the null-safety check a runtime workaround rather than a type-safe solution.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add proper TypeScript type annotations, e.g., `items: { price: number }[]` or define an interface. Consider also making the parameter optional with `items?: { price: number }[]` if the function should handle undefined.

producedFindings.title: Incomplete input validation
producedFindings.description: The check `if (!items)` only guards against null/undefined/empty values but does not validate that `items` is an array. If a non-array object with a truthy value is passed, `reduce` will throw a TypeError.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using `if (!Array.isArray(items)) return 0;` for more robust validation, or better yet, rely on TypeScript types to enforce correct usage at compile time.

producedFindings.title: Potential runtime error from null/undefined array elements
producedFindings.description: The reduce callback accesses b.price without checking if b is truthy or if price exists. If the array contains null/undefined elements or objects without a price property, this will throw a runtime error.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add defensive checks in the reduce callback, e.g., 'items.reduce((a, b) => a + (b?.price ?? 0), 0)' or validate items before processing.
